---
title: "The Visa That Built Silicon Valley"
date: 2025-01-05
subtitle: "\"Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free.\" — Emma Lazarus"
description: "In late December 2024, a fierce debate erupted in America about the H-1B visa — a programme that allows US companies to hire specialised workers from abroad. Elon Musk and Vivek Ramaswamy defended it passionately. Other Trump supporters wanted it abolished. Behind this political fight is a real economic question: what does skilled immigration actually do for a country?"
image: "./title_20250105.png"
twitter-card: {image: "./title_20250105.png"}
open-graph: {image: "./title_20250105.png"}
categories: ["Economy & Policy"]
author: "Satvik Raman"
---
<p><b>Pageviews:</b> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="1em" height="1em" viewBox="0 0 256 256"><path fill="currentColor" d="M247.31 124.76c-.35-.79-8.82-19.58-27.65-38.41C194.57 61.26 162.88 48 128 48S61.43 61.26 36.34 86.35C17.51 105.18 9 124 8.69 124.76a8 8 0 0 0 0 6.5c.35.79 8.82 19.57 27.65 38.4C61.43 194.74 93.12 208 128 208s66.57-13.26 91.66-38.34c18.83-18.83 27.3-37.61 27.65-38.4a8 8 0 0 0 0-6.5M128 168a40 40 0 1 1 40-40a40 40 0 0 1-40 40"/></svg> <span class="waline-pageview-count"></i><p>

# Introduction

In late December 2024, an unlikely debate exploded across American social media. The trigger was a question about whether highly skilled immigrants were taking jobs from American workers. Elon Musk, who himself immigrated from South Africa and co-founded PayPal before building Tesla and SpaceX, defended the programme vigorously and said he would fight to protect it. Vivek Ramaswamy, the son of Indian immigrants, joined him. On the other side, some supporters of Donald Trump's incoming administration argued that the H-1B visa was a mechanism for corporations to suppress American wages by importing cheaper foreign labour. The argument became particularly pointed because many of the programme's most prominent defenders were themselves recent immigrants who had risen to extraordinary prominence in American business. And India, which sends more H-1B visa holders to the United States than any other country, was watching closely.

# What the H-1B Visa Actually Is

The H-1B visa is a non-immigrant visa that allows American employers to temporarily hire foreign workers in speciality occupations that require at least a bachelor's degree or the equivalent. It was created in 1990, when the United States government recognised that American universities and training programmes were not producing enough workers with certain technical and scientific skills to meet the needs of the expanding technology industry. The visa has a maximum duration of three years, which can be extended for another three years, and recipients must be sponsored by a US employer. The United States government issues approximately eighty-five thousand new H-1B visas each year, split between a general pool and a set-aside for people holding advanced degrees from American universities. These numbers are far lower than the demand, which means the visas are allocated partly by lottery. In a typical year, the number of applications exceeds the available visas by a ratio of several to one.

# India's Enormous Stake

India is by far the largest beneficiary of the H-1B programme. Indian nationals receive approximately seventy percent of all H-1B visas issued each year. This is not simply the result of aggressive lobbying or corporate preferences. It reflects the significant investment India has made over decades in engineering and technical education, particularly through the Indian Institutes of Technology and other institutions that produce graduates who compete effectively for roles in American technology companies. The Indian-American professional community has become one of the most economically successful immigrant groups in American history. Sundar Pichai, the chief executive of Google, and Satya Nadella, the chief executive of Microsoft, are both Indian-born and have held their positions in part because of pathways that began with education and immigration. Beyond individual success stories, remittances from Indian workers in the United States contribute meaningfully to India's foreign exchange earnings. Any significant change to the H-1B programme would affect hundreds of thousands of Indian professionals currently working in the United States, as well as millions more who aspire to do so.

# The Arguments on Both Sides

The case for the H-1B visa rests on the idea that talent is scarce and geographically distributed. The United States needs certain technical skills, and restricting where companies can find those skills reduces the overall output of the economy. American technology companies have argued consistently that they cannot fill their open positions with American workers alone, not because American workers are less capable but because the supply of people with the specific combination of skills they need is limited. The case against the H-1B programme, at least in its current form, focuses on two concerns. First, some employers have been accused of using H-1B visas to replace American workers at lower wages, exploiting the fact that H-1B holders are legally tied to their sponsoring employer and therefore have little bargaining power. Second, critics argue that the programme, by making foreign technical labour available at scale, reduces the pressure on companies to train American workers or raise wages for existing American workers.

# Final Thoughts

The H-1B debate is, at its core, a debate about what immigration is for. If a country's goal is to maximise total economic output, then attracting the best available talent from anywhere in the world is a straightforward strategy. If the goal is to maximise the welfare of existing citizens, the calculation becomes more complicated, because the benefits of skilled immigration are distributed broadly but the costs, if any, tend to be concentrated among workers who compete directly with incoming immigrants. India's interest in this debate is significant but also limited. The decisions will be made in Washington by politicians responding to American voters, not by the millions of Indian engineers and professionals who have built careers around the programme's existence. For them, as for anyone watching a policy debate about something that directly affects their life, the lesson is that no visa category, no immigration programme, and no career pathway is permanently safe from political reconsideration.
